description: This is a real, valid Renovate config for this repo — not a demo prop. It's what would actually open the version-bump PR in production, running independently of and after any Lightwell-driven emergency patch (see scripts/simulate-dependency-pr.sh for what that PR looks like without needing Renovate wired up to a live GitHub account). packageRules below distinguish a genuine security backport/z-stream fix from a routine feature-carrying update — see the comments on each rule.

packageRules.description: Lightwell/Red Hat-backported security fixes, identified by the build qualifier on the version Renovate is proposing to move TO — not by what the version jump looks like under semver. '.redhat-NNNNN' is Red Hat's long-established Maven qualifier for exactly this kind of backport (real prior art: renovatebot/renovate discussion #9346, where Red Hat customers already configure Renovate around this exact suffix). Lightwell's own final qualifier format is still to be confirmed — see docs/ARCHITECTURE.md — so this pattern also matches the '.rhlw-NNNNN' form seen in early Lightwell UI screenshots, until that's settled. These are backports to your PINNED version, not forward releases, so they get top priority regardless of how big the version number jump looks.

Non-Lightwell dependencies: genuine patch/z-stream-level security fixes (via Renovate's vulnerabilityAlerts, which ties to actual CVE data, not just version-number shape). Still high priority, no schedule restriction — but this is the fallback for packages Lightwell doesn't cover, not the primary path.

Routine (non-security) minor/major updates — these are the ones likely to carry new features, not just fixes. Batch and review on a normal cadence; never top priority, and never matched by the two rules above even if a project mislabels a feature release as a patch bump.

prBodyNotes: If this PR bumps a library that was already patched via Project Lightwell's module-swap pipeline, this commit is non-blocking paperwork — it brings source control in line with what's already running. It does not gate or delay the security fix itself.

If this PR's target version does NOT carry a Red Hat/Lightwell qualifier, it's either a non-Lightwell dependency's own security patch, or a routine update that may include new functionality — check the changelog before treating it as equivalent to a backport.
